title: CQRS Light
content: ## What and Why
Actions separates every database interaction into either a **Query** (read) or
a **Command** (write). They are distinct classes with distinct responsibilities,
but they share the same database and the same schema.
This is "CQRS Light" — not full CQRS. There are no separate read/write stores,
no event sourcing, and no eventual consistency. The separation is purely at the
class level.
## Architectural Benefits
**Clear separation of concerns.** A query never mutates state. A command never
returns a paginated grid. When you open a file, you know immediately whether it
reads or writes.
**Optimized query paths.** Reads can disable change tracking and use projections
for display performance. Writes use full change tracking for correctness. The
separation allows each path to be tuned independently.
**Audit-friendly write paths.** Every mutation flows through a command, making it
straightforward to apply audit logging uniformly. There is no "accidental write"
hidden in a query.
**Proven lineage.** Capstone and Identity established this pattern. Actions
adopts the same structure, reducing onboarding friction for developers who move
between projects.
## Separation Principle
QUERIES                              COMMANDS
Read-only                            Mutation + side effects
Optimized for display                Optimized for correctness
No business rules                    Business rules + validation
No audit logging                     Audit logging on success
Cacheable                            Cache invalidation
This separation is enforced by convention, not by infrastructure. The benefit of
convention over enforcement is zero infrastructure overhead and easy debugging.
## Decision: Why Not Full CQRS?
Full CQRS introduces a separate read store, an event bus to synchronize it, and
eventual consistency between stores. This suits high-scale distributed systems
with fundamentally different read and write access patterns.
Actions is a single-service platform with a single PostgreSQL database. Read and
write models share the same schema. There is no cross-service event choreography
and no reason to accept the complexity of eventual consistency.
CQRS Light gives Actions the primary benefit of CQRS — clean separation of
concerns — without the infrastructure overhead.
If scale demands it later, read replicas can be introduced behind the query
classes without changing command code. The separation boundary is already in
place; only the data source would change.
